subject: Chemistry
q: A solution of two miscible liquids showing negative deviation from Raoult's law will have :
question: A solution of two miscible liquids showing negative deviation from Raoult's law will have :
options: increased vapour pressure, increased boiling point
increased vapour pressure, decreased boiling point
decreased vapour pressure, decreased boiling point
decreased vapour pressure, increased boiling point
solution:
Raoult's law states that a solvent's partial vapour pressure in a solution (or mixture) is equal or identical to the vapour pressure of the pure solvent multiplied by its mole fraction in the solution.

The compounds which show negative deviation from Raoult's law are a mixture of acetone and chloroform. This results in the formation of the hydrogen bonding between atoms of molecules. There is a decrease in the vapour pressure of the mixture that results in a negative deviation from Raoult's law.

Solution with negative deviation has
 PT< PA0XA + PB0XB  PA< PA0XA  PB< PB0XB
 If vapour pressure decreases, so the boiling point increases.

subject: Chemistry
q: If the degree of dissociation of aqueous solution of weak monobasic acid is determined to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn></math>, then the observed freezing point will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math> higher than the expected/theoretical freezing point. (Nearest integer).
question: If the degree of dissociation of aqueous solution of weak monobasic acid is determined to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn></math>, then the observed freezing point will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math> higher than the expected/theoretical freezing point. (Nearest integer).
solution:
HA1-α⇋H+α+A-α

Van't Hoff factor, i=1+αn-1

                            =1+0.32-1

                            =1+0.3

                            =1.3

∆Tf without considering dissociation =kf× molality

∆Tf without considering dissociation =ikf× molality

=ΔTf'

ΔTf'-ΔTf=0.3 (kf× molality)

ΔTf1-ΔTfΔTf=0.3kf×molalitykf×molality=0.3

% value =30

subject: Chemistry
q:
At $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K}), 100 \mathrm{~g}$ of $98 \% \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w})$ aqueous solution is mixed with 100 g of $49 \% \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w})$ aqueous solution. What is the mole fraction of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ in the resultant solution?
(Given : Atomic mass $\mathrm{H}=1 \mathrm{u} ; \mathrm{S}=32 \mathrm{u} ; \mathrm{O}=16 \mathrm{u}$).
(Assume that temperature after mixing remains constant)
question:
At $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K}), 100 \mathrm{~g}$ of $98 \% \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w})$ aqueous solution is mixed with 100 g of $49 \% \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w})$ aqueous solution. What is the mole fraction of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ in the resultant solution?
(Given : Atomic mass $\mathrm{H}=1 \mathrm{u} ; \mathrm{S}=32 \mathrm{u} ; \mathrm{O}=16 \mathrm{u}$).
(Assume that temperature after mixing remains constant)
solution:
Molar mass of H₂SO₄ = 98 g/mol.
Solution 1: 100 g at 98% gives 98 g H₂SO₄ = 1 mol; water = 2 g = 0.111 mol.
Solution 2: 100 g at 49% gives 49 g H₂SO₄ = 0.5 mol; water = 51 g = 2.833 mol.
Mixed solution: Total H₂SO₄ = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5 mol.
Total water = 0.111 + 2.833 = 2.944 mol.
Mole fraction of H₂SO₄ = 1.5/(1.5 + 2.944) = 1.5/4.444 = 0.337.

question:

[Given : Kf=1.86 K kg mol-1; Density of water =1.00 g cm-3; Freezing point of water =273.15 K
solution:
Molality =40180mol0.2Kg=109 molal

⇒ΔTf=Tf-Tf'=1.86×109

⇒Tf'=273.15-1.86×109

=271.08 K

≈271 K (nearest-integer)

subject: Chemistry
q: For the solution of the gases <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> in water at <math><mn>298</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the Henry's law constants <math><mo>(</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow></msub><mo>)</mo></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.5,</mn><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>35</mn></math> and <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. The correct plot for the given data is:
question: For the solution of the gases <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> in water at <math><mn>298</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the Henry's law constants <math><mo>(</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow></msub><mo>)</mo></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.5,</mn><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>35</mn></math> and <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. The correct plot for the given data is:
solution:
Pgas=KH.Xgas

Where, Pgas is the partial pressure of gas.

KH is the Henry’s constant

Xgas is the mole fraction of gas

Pgas=KH1-XH2O

Pgas=KH-KHXH2O

On comparing this with the equation of straight line, y=mx+c, the intercept, c=KH. Thus, the gas having more value of Henry's law constant will have more value of intercept on the y-axis.

subject: Chemistry
q: If the boiling points of two solvents <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> (having same molecular weights) are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>2: 1</mtext></math> and their enthalpy of vaporizations are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></math>, then the boiling point elevation constant of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> times the boiling point elevation constant of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is (nearest integer).
question: If the boiling points of two solvents <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> (having same molecular weights) are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>2: 1</mtext></math> and their enthalpy of vaporizations are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></math>, then the boiling point elevation constant of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> times the boiling point elevation constant of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is (nearest integer).
solution:
The boiling point elevation constant Kb of a volatile liquid is given by the following expression
Kb=RTb2MΔHv

Where Tb=boiling point of the liquid
            M=molar mass of the liquid
            ΔHv= Enthalpy of vaporisation

The boiling points of the two solvents are in the ratio 2:1, which means that the boiling point elevation constants are also in the ratio 2:1. Let's call the boiling point elevation constant of solvent X as (Tb)x, and the boiling point elevation constant of solvent Y as (Tb)y.
Given: Mx=My
Tbx=2Tby
2ΔHvx=ΔHvy
∴ KbxKby=212×21=81=m
∴ m=8

Therefore, the value of m is 8(nearest integer).

q: The vapour pressures of two volatile liquids <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> Torr and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> Torr, respectively. If the liquid mixture contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn></math> mole fraction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, then the mole fraction of liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> in the vapour phase is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>17</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
question: The vapour pressures of two volatile liquids <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> Torr and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> Torr, respectively. If the liquid mixture contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn></math> mole fraction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, then the mole fraction of liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> in the vapour phase is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>17</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
solution:
Since,

yA=p°A×xAp°A×xA+p°B×xB

and xA=0.3 and xB=0.7

yA=50×0.350×0.3+100×0.7

yA=1585.

YB1-yB=PB0PA0XB1-XB

⇒yB1-yB=100500.70.3=143

⇒yB=1417

(R=0.08206 L atm K-1 mol-1)
question:

(R=0.08206 L atm K-1 mol-1)
solution:
i factor of glucose and urea are 1 : 1 respectively.

Moles of glucose =weightMolecular wieght=1.8180=10-2

Moles of urea =0.660=10-2

Total mole of solute =10-2+10-2=2×10-2

Concentration of solution =2×10-2100×10-3=0.2

Osmotic pressure (π)=CRT

=0.2×0.0821×300=4.926 atm

subject: Chemistry
q:
20% of acetic acid is dissociated when its 5 g is added to 500 mL of water. The depression in freezing point of such water is _____×10-3C∘. Atomic mass of C,H and O are 12, 1 and 16 a.m.u. respectively.

[Given : Molal depression constant and density of water are 1.86   K kg mol-1 and 1 g cm-3 respectively.
question:
20% of acetic acid is dissociated when its 5 g is added to 500 mL of water. The depression in freezing point of such water is _____×10-3C∘. Atomic mass of C,H and O are 12, 1 and 16 a.m.u. respectively.

[Given : Molal depression constant and density of water are 1.86   K kg mol-1 and 1 g cm-3 respectively.
solution:
Van't Hoff factor (i) for dissociation of acetic acid can be written as follows,

i=1+(n-1)α

n=Number of ions

α=Degree of dissociation

(i=1+0.2(2-1)=1.2

ΔTf=iKfmΔTf=Depression in freezing pointKf=Molar depression constantm=molality

ΔTf=1.2×1.86×5×100060×500

ΔTf=0.372

ΔTf=372×10-3

subject: Chemistry
q:
If $10^{-4} \mathrm{dm}^3$ of water is introduced into a $1.0 \mathrm{dm}^3$ flask to $300 \mathrm{~K}$, how many moles of water are in the vapour phase when equilibrium is established?
(Given : Vapour pressure of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ is $3170 \mathrm{~Pa} ; \mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
question:
If $10^{-4} \mathrm{dm}^3$ of water is introduced into a $1.0 \mathrm{dm}^3$ flask to $300 \mathrm{~K}$, how many moles of water are in the vapour phase when equilibrium is established?
(Given : Vapour pressure of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ is $3170 \mathrm{~Pa} ; \mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
solution:
$\mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{PV}}{\mathrm{RT}}=$
$=128 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{moles}$
$=\frac{3170 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~atm} \times 1 \mathrm{~L}}{0.0821 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{\textrm {k } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { mol } ^ { - 1 } \times 3 0 0 \mathrm { K }}} \approx 1.27 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol}$

The normal boiling point of the sea water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts/><none/><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts></math> (Nearest integer) Assume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>%</mo></math> ionization for both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>MgCl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> Given: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>52</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>MgCl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>58</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>95</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively.
question: Sea water contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>29</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo><mi>NaCl</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>19</mn><mo>%</mo><msub><mi>MgCl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> by weight of solution. The normal boiling point of the sea water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts/><none/><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts></math> (Nearest integer) Assume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>%</mo></math> ionization for both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>MgCl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> Given: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>52</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> Molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>MgCl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>58</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>95</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively.
solution:
Molality of solution=moles of solutemass of solvent×1000

Number of moles NaCl =29.2558.5=0.5

Number of moles of MgCl2=1995=0.2

NaCl=0.551.75×1000

MgCl2=0.251.75×1000

ΔTb=i1 m1+i2 m2kb

∆Tb= Elevation in boiling point

i=Van't Hoff factor

m = molality

kb = molal elevation constant

∆Tb=2×0.5×100051.75×3×0.2×100051.75×0.52

= 16.077

Boiling point of sea water

=116.077°C

≈116°C (Nearest integer)

subject: Chemistry
q:
When 1 g each of compounds AB and $\mathrm{AB}_2$ are dissolved in 15 g of water separately, they increased the boiling point of water by 2.7 K and 1.5 K respectively. The atomic mass of A (in amu) is ____ $\times 10^{-1}$ (Nearest integer)
(Given : Molal boiling point elevation constant is $\left.0.5 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
question:
When 1 g each of compounds AB and $\mathrm{AB}_2$ are dissolved in 15 g of water separately, they increased the boiling point of water by 2.7 K and 1.5 K respectively. The atomic mass of A (in amu) is ____ $\times 10^{-1}$ (Nearest integer)
(Given : Molal boiling point elevation constant is $\left.0.5 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
solution:
For AB
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=2.7 \mathrm{~K} \\
& 2.7=1 \times 0.5 \times \mathrm{m} \\
& \mathrm{~m}=\frac{27}{5}
\end{aligned}$
Let molar mass of $\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{x}$.
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { So } \frac{1 / x}{15} \times 1000=\frac{27}{5} \\
& x=12.34
\end{aligned}$
For $\mathrm{AB}_2$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=1.5 \mathrm{~K} \\
& 1.5=1 \times 0.5 \times \mathrm{m} \\
& \mathrm{~m}=3
\end{aligned}$
Let molar mass of $\mathrm{AB}_2=\mathrm{y}$
So $\frac{1 / \mathrm{y}}{15} \times 1000=3$
$\begin{aligned}
& y=\frac{1000}{45} \\
& y=22.22
\end{aligned}$
Now let a and b be atomic masses of A and B respectively, then
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{b}=12.34 \quad...(i)\\
& \mathrm{~A}+2 \mathrm{~b}=22.22 \quad...(ii) \\
& \mathrm{~B}=22.22-12.34=9.88 \\
& \text { Now } \mathrm{a}=12.34-9.88=2.46 \\
& =24.6 \times 10^{-1}=25 \times 10^{-1}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry

solution:

24=(1)×1.86×W62×18.6

=14.880 kg

solution:

subject: Chemistry
q:
A mixture of ethyl alcohol and propyl alcohol has a vapour pressure of $290 \mathrm{~mm}$ at $300 \mathrm{~K}$. 
The vapour pressure of propyl alcohol is $200 \mathrm{~mm}$. If the mole fraction of ethyl alcohol is $0.6$, its vapour pressure (in $\mathrm{mm}$) at the same temperature will be
question:
A mixture of ethyl alcohol and propyl alcohol has a vapour pressure of $290 \mathrm{~mm}$ at $300 \mathrm{~K}$. 
The vapour pressure of propyl alcohol is $200 \mathrm{~mm}$. If the mole fraction of ethyl alcohol is $0.6$, its vapour pressure (in $\mathrm{mm}$) at the same temperature will be
solution:
Let the vapour pressure of pure ethyl alcohol be $\mathrm{P}$,
According to Raoult's law
$\begin{aligned}
& 290=200 \times 0.4+\mathrm{P} \times 0.6 \\
& \mathrm{P}=\frac{290-80}{0.6}=350 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~Hg}
\end{aligned}$
Hence, (A) is correct.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The total pressure of a mixture of non-reacting gases X(0.6 g) and Y(0.45 g) in a vessel is 740 mm of Hg. The partial pressure of the gas X is mm of Hg. (Nearest Integer)
(Given : molar mass X=20 and Y=45 g mol-1 )
question:
The total pressure of a mixture of non-reacting gases X(0.6 g) and Y(0.45 g) in a vessel is 740 mm of Hg. The partial pressure of the gas X is mm of Hg. (Nearest Integer)
(Given : molar mass X=20 and Y=45 g mol-1 )
solution:
Total pressure (PTotal) = 740 mm of Hg

Number of moles of gas X = 0.620

Number of moles of gas Y = 0.4545

Total number of moles = 0.03 + 0.01 = 0.04 mole

The partial pressure of the gas X = Mole fraction × Total pressure 

X = 0.030.04 × 740 = 555

subject: Chemistry
q: The degree of dissociation $(\alpha)$ of a weak electrolyte, $A_x B_y$ is related to van't Hoff factor (i) by the expression:
question: The degree of dissociation $(\alpha)$ of a weak electrolyte, $A_x B_y$ is related to van't Hoff factor (i) by the expression:
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& i=1-\alpha+n \alpha=1+\alpha(n-1) \\
& \frac{i-1}{n-1}=\alpha \\
& A_x B_y \rightarrow x A^{+y}+y B^{-x} \\
& n=x+y
\end{aligned}
$$
So $\alpha=\frac{i-1}{x+y-1}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Solute A associates in water. When 0.7 g of solute A is dissolved in 42.0 g of water, it depresses the freezing point by 0.2 °C. The percentage association of solute A in water, is

[Given: Molar mass of A=93 g mol-1. Molal depression constant of water is 1.86 K kg mol-1]
question:
Solute A associates in water. When 0.7 g of solute A is dissolved in 42.0 g of water, it depresses the freezing point by 0.2 °C. The percentage association of solute A in water, is

[Given: Molar mass of A=93 g mol-1. Molal depression constant of water is 1.86 K kg mol-1]
solution:
Depression in freezing point,ΔTf=iKf×m

2.0=ix1.86×7×100093×42

2.0=i3.33

i=0.6

i=1+1n-1β

0.6=1+12-1β

β=0.8

%β=80

Hence, the percentage association of solute A in water is 80.

subject: Chemistry
q: The elevation in boiling point for <math><mn>1</mn></math> molal solution of glucose is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The depression in freezing point for <math><mn>2</mn></math> molal solution of glucose in the same solvent is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub></math> is:
question: The elevation in boiling point for <math><mn>1</mn></math> molal solution of glucose is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The depression in freezing point for <math><mn>2</mn></math> molal solution of glucose in the same solvent is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The relation between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub></math> is:
solution:
Freezing point depression refers to the lowering of the freezing point of solvent upon the addition of solute. Boiling point elevation refers to the increase in the boiling point of a solvent upon the addition of a solute.

∆Tb=iKbm⇒2=iKb×1............i

∆Tf=iKfm⇒2=iKf×2...............ii

Dividing iii,

1=Kb2Kf

Kb=2 Kf

subject: Chemistry
q:
' W ' g of a non-volatile electrolyte solid solute of molar mass ' M ' $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ when dissolved in 100 mL water, decreases vapour pressure of water from 640 mm Hg to 600 mm Hg. If aqueous solution of the electrolyte boils at 375 K and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ for water is $0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, then the mole fraction of the electrolyte solute $\left(x_{2}\right)$ in the solution can be expressed as
(Given : density of water $=1 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$ and boiling point of water $=373 \mathrm{~K}$)
question:
' W ' g of a non-volatile electrolyte solid solute of molar mass ' M ' $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ when dissolved in 100 mL water, decreases vapour pressure of water from 640 mm Hg to 600 mm Hg. If aqueous solution of the electrolyte boils at 375 K and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ for water is $0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, then the mole fraction of the electrolyte solute $\left(x_{2}\right)$ in the solution can be expressed as
(Given : density of water $=1 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$ and boiling point of water $=373 \mathrm{~K}$)
solution:
Given the vapour pressure of pure water $P^o = 640$ mm Hg and vapour pressure of solution $P_s = 600$ mm Hg.
According to Raoult's law for an electrolyte solution, the relative lowering of vapour pressure is given by $\frac{P^o - P_s}{P^o} = i \cdot x_2$, where $i$ is the van't Hoff factor and $x_2$ is the mole fraction of the solute.
Substituting the values: $\frac{640 - 600}{640} = i \cdot x_2$
$\frac{40}{640} = i \cdot x_2 \implies i \cdot x_2 = \frac{1}{16}$ ... (1)
The elevation in boiling point is given by $\Delta T_b = i \cdot K_b \cdot m$, where $m$ is the molality.
Given $T_b = 375$ K and $T_b^o = 373$ K, so $\Delta T_b = 375 - 373 = 2$ K.
Molality $m = \frac{W}{M \times 100} \times 1000 = \frac{10W}{M}$ (since mass of water = $100$ mL $\times 1$ g/mL = $100$ g).
Substituting in the elevation formula: $2 = i \cdot 0.52 \cdot \frac{10W}{M}$
$i = \frac{2M}{5.2W} = \frac{20M}{52W} = \frac{5M}{13W}$ ... (2)
Substitute the value of $i$ from (2) into (1):
$\frac{5M}{13W} \cdot x_2 = \frac{1}{16}$
$x_2 = \frac{1}{16} \cdot \frac{13W}{5M} = \frac{13}{80} \cdot \frac{W}{M}$
This can be rewritten as $x_2 = \frac{1.3}{8} \times \frac{W}{M}$.

subject: Chemistry
q: Liquids <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> form an ideal solution in the entire composition range. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>350</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the vapour pressure of pure A and pure B are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>Pa</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>Pa</mi></math> , respectively. The composition of the vapour in equilibrium with a solution containing 40 mole percent of A at this temperature is:
question: Liquids <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> form an ideal solution in the entire composition range. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>350</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the vapour pressure of pure A and pure B are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>Pa</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>Pa</mi></math> , respectively. The composition of the vapour in equilibrium with a solution containing 40 mole percent of A at this temperature is:
solution:
Since, PA0=7×103Pa; PB0=12×103Pa and XA=0.4,  X B =1−0.4=0.6

According to Raoult's Law: PT=PA0XA+PB0XB

PT=7×1030.4+12×1030.6

PT=10×103Pa

Now, PA0XA=PTxA

xA=PA0XAPT=7×103×0.4104⇒0.28

xB=1-xA⇒ 1-0.28⇒0.72

subject: Chemistry
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> molal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mi>Fe</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>CN</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>6</mn></msub></math> solution has a degree of dissociation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo></math> Its boiling point is equal to that of another solution which contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn></math> weight percent of a non electrolytic solute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is _____ u. (Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Density of water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="" close="]"><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> molal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mi>Fe</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>CN</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>6</mn></msub></math> solution has a degree of dissociation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo></math> Its boiling point is equal to that of another solution which contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn></math> weight percent of a non electrolytic solute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The molar mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is _____ u. (Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Density of water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="" close="]"><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
K4Fe(CN)6     ⇌     4 K+  +    Fe(CN)64-

 Initial conc. 1 m              0               0

 Final conc. (1-0.4)m      4×0.4m  0.4 m

                =0.6 m             =1.6 m      =0.4 m

Effective molality =0.6+1.6+0.4=2.6 m

For same boiling point, the molality of another solution should also be 2.6 m.

Now, 18.1 weight percent solution means

18.1gm solute is present in 100gm solution and hence, (100-18.1=)81.9gm water. Now, 2.6=18.1/M81.9/1000

∴ Molar mass of solute, M=85

question:

subject: Chemistry
q:
The number of units, which are used to express concentration of solutions from the following is _________.

(Mass percent, Mole, Mole fraction, Molarity, ppm, Molality.)
question:
The number of units, which are used to express concentration of solutions from the following is _________.

(Mass percent, Mole, Mole fraction, Molarity, ppm, Molality.)
solution: Both solute and solvent(solution) terms are involved in the concentration term of solution. Mass percent, mole fraction, molarity, ppm, molality are used for measuring concentration terms. The number of moles is mass to molar mass ratio, it is not a concentration term for a solution.

subject: Chemistry
q: $19.5$ g of fluoro acetic acid (molar mass $= 78$ g mol$^{-1}$) is dissolved in $500$ g of water at $298$ K. The depression in the freezing point of water was $1°$C. What is $K_a$ of fluoro acetic acid? (For water, $K_f = 1.86$ K kg mol$^{-1}$). Assume molarity and molality to have same values.
question: $19.5$ g of fluoro acetic acid (molar mass $= 78$ g mol$^{-1}$) is dissolved in $500$ g of water at $298$ K. The depression in the freezing point of water was $1°$C. What is $K_a$ of fluoro acetic acid? (For water, $K_f = 1.86$ K kg mol$^{-1}$). Assume molarity and molality to have same values.
solution:
The molality of the solution is given by:

$m = \dfrac{\text{Mass of solute}}{\text{Molar mass of solute} \times \text{Mass of solvent in kg}}$

$m = \dfrac{19.5}{78 \times 0.5} = 0.5 \text{ mol kg}^{-1}$

The depression in freezing point is given by:

$\Delta T_f = i \times K_f \times m$

Substituting the given values:

$1 = i \times 1.86 \times 0.5$

$1 = i \times 0.93$

$i = \dfrac{1}{0.93} = \dfrac{100}{93}$

For the dissociation of a weak acid $HA \rightleftharpoons H^+ + A^-$, the van 't Hoff factor is $i = 1 + \alpha$, where $\alpha$ is the degree of dissociation.

$1 + \alpha = \dfrac{100}{93}$

$\alpha = \dfrac{100}{93} - 1 = \dfrac{7}{93}$

The acid dissociation constant $K_a$ is given by:

$K_a = \dfrac{C \alpha^2}{1 - \alpha}$

Since molarity and molality have the same values, $C = 0.5 \text{ M}$.

$K_a = \dfrac{0.5 \times \left(\dfrac{7}{93}\right)^2}{1 - \dfrac{7}{93}}$

$K_a = \dfrac{0.5 \times \dfrac{49}{93^2}}{\dfrac{86}{93}} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 49}{93 \times 86}$

$K_a = \dfrac{24.5}{7998} \approx 3.06 \times 10^{-3}$

The closest value is $3 \times 10^{-3}$.

Answer: $3 \times 10^{-3}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
A 300mL bottle of soft drink has 0.2MCO2 dissolved in it. Assuming CO2 behaves as an ideal gas, the volume of the dissolved CO2 at STP is _____ mL. (Nearest integer)

Given: At STP, molar volume of an ideal gas is 22.7L mol-1
question:
A 300mL bottle of soft drink has 0.2MCO2 dissolved in it. Assuming CO2 behaves as an ideal gas, the volume of the dissolved CO2 at STP is _____ mL. (Nearest integer)

Given: At STP, molar volume of an ideal gas is 22.7L mol-1
solution:
Moles dissolved in soft drinks of CO2=molarity × volume = 0.2M×300×10-3L

=0.06 Mole 

Volume of 0.06 mole of CO2 at S.T.P

=0.06×22.7

=1.362 L

subject: Chemistry
q:
Assume a living cell with $0.9 \%(\omega / \omega)$ of glucose solution (aqueous). This cell is immersed in another solution having equal mole fraction of glucose and water.
(Consider the data upto first decimal place only)
The cell will :
question:
Assume a living cell with $0.9 \%(\omega / \omega)$ of glucose solution (aqueous). This cell is immersed in another solution having equal mole fraction of glucose and water.
(Consider the data upto first decimal place only)
The cell will :
options: show no change in volume since solution is $0.9 \%(\omega / \omega)$
solution:
Living cell $=0.9 \mathrm{gm}$ in 100 gm of solution
$\% \mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w}=0.9$

Solution is have equal moles of glucose and water $=0.5$
Weight of solution $=0.5 \times 180+0.5 \times 18=99 \mathrm{gm}$ $\% \mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w} \simeq 90 \%$
Concentrated solution = Cell will shrink.

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> molal solution of a weak acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HA</mi></math> has a freezing point of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>885</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The degree of dissociation of this acid is ______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> (Round off to the Nearest Integer). <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo></math>Given : Molal depression constant of water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> Freezing point of pure water<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="" close="]"><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> molal solution of a weak acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HA</mi></math> has a freezing point of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>885</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The degree of dissociation of this acid is ______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> (Round off to the Nearest Integer). <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo></math>Given : Molal depression constant of water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> Freezing point of pure water<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="" close="]"><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
ΔTf=(1+α)Kf×m3.885 = (1+α)×1.85 ×2

α=0.05=50×10-3

subject: Chemistry
q: Vapour pressure of pure benzene is 119 torr and that of toluene is $37.0$ torr at the same temperature. Mole fraction of toluene in vapour phase which is in equilibrium with a solution of benzene and toluene having a mole fraction of toluene $0.50$, will be :
question: Vapour pressure of pure benzene is 119 torr and that of toluene is $37.0$ torr at the same temperature. Mole fraction of toluene in vapour phase which is in equilibrium with a solution of benzene and toluene having a mole fraction of toluene $0.50$, will be :
solution:
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^0 \times x_{\mathrm{A}}=$ total pressure $\times \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{A}}$
$$
\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^0 \times x_{\mathrm{B}}=\text { total pressure } \times \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{B}}
$$
where $\mathrm{x}$ and $\mathrm{y}$ represents mole fraction in liquid and vapour phase respectively.
$$
\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^0 x_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^0 x_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{A}}} ; \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^0\left(1-x_{\mathrm{A}}\right)}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^0 x_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{1-\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{A}}}
$$
on putting values $\frac{119(1-0.50)}{37 \times 0.50}=\frac{1-y_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{A}}}$ on solving $\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{A}}=0.237$

subject: Chemistry
q:
When 9.45 g of ClCH2COOH is added to 500 mL of water, its freezing point drops by 0.5°C. The dissociation constant of ClCH2COOH is x×10-3. The value of x is off to the nearest integer)

KfH2O=1.86 K kg mol-1
question:
When 9.45 g of ClCH2COOH is added to 500 mL of water, its freezing point drops by 0.5°C. The dissociation constant of ClCH2COOH is x×10-3. The value of x is off to the nearest integer)

KfH2O=1.86 K kg mol-1
solution:
ClCH2COOH⇌ClCH2COO⊖+H+

i=1+(2-1)α

i=1+α

ΔTf=i.Kf.m

0.5=(1+α)(1.86)9.4594.55001000

53.72=1+α⇒α=1.283.72

α=3293

Ka=Cα2C−Cα=Cα21−αC=0.1500/1000=0.2

Ka=0.232/9321−32/93=0.2×32293×61

=0.036

Ka=36×10−3

subject: Chemistry
q: If a substance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>'</mo></math> dissolves in solution of a mixture of ' <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>' and ' <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> ' with their respective number of moles as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>n</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>n</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>n</mi><mi>C</mi></msub></math>, mole fraction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> in the solution is:
question: If a substance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>'</mo></math> dissolves in solution of a mixture of ' <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>' and ' <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> ' with their respective number of moles as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>n</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>n</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>n</mi><mi>C</mi></msub></math>, mole fraction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> in the solution is:
solution:
Mole fraction is defined as the amount (number of moles or molecules) of a constituent or component divided by the total amount of all the constituent or component present. 

Mole fraction=Number of moles of compnentTotal number of moles of all the components
Suppose we have a mixture with 3 components A, B and C.

Mole fraction of C=nCnA+nB+nC

Hence, the answer is option B.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The molarity of the solution prepared by dissolving 6.3 g of oxalic acid H2C2O4·2H2O in 250 mL of water in mol L-1 is x×10-2. The value of x is _________ . (Nearest integer)
[Atomic mass : H : 1.0, C : 12.0, 0 : 16.0 J]
question:
The molarity of the solution prepared by dissolving 6.3 g of oxalic acid H2C2O4·2H2O in 250 mL of water in mol L-1 is x×10-2. The value of x is _________ . (Nearest integer)
[Atomic mass : H : 1.0, C : 12.0, 0 : 16.0 J]
solution:
M=Wsolute×1000GMMsolute×Vml

=6.3×1000126×250=0.2 M
=20×10-2
So X=20

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : NaCl is added to the ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, present in the ice cream box to prevent the melting of ice cream.
Statement (II) : On addition of NaCl to ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, there is a depression in freezing point.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : NaCl is added to the ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, present in the ice cream box to prevent the melting of ice cream.
Statement (II) : On addition of NaCl to ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, there is a depression in freezing point.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution: A mixture of salt and ice is known as freezing mixture. Freezing mixture decreases freezing point of ice. Both statements are true.
